## 14 · Broader Impact and Future Directions

With a trained classifier in hand, we now score every edge in the crosswalk graph and ask: where do the predictions point compliance teams toward new mappings?

# Figure 14.1 — Two-panel: predicted tier distribution + confidence histograms
import json as _json14

# Load the 4,001 scored edges
edge_preds = _json14.loads(
    (REPO / "runs" / "vfinal" / "edge_predictions.json").read_text()
)

# Flatten to lists
pred_tiers  = [v["tier"] for v in edge_preds.values()]
pred_names  = [v["tier_name"] for v in edge_preds.values()]
pred_confs  = [v["confidence"] for v in edge_preds.values()]

print(f"Total scored edges:   {len(pred_tiers):,}")
non_trivial = sum(1 for t in pred_tiers if t > 0)
print(f"Non-trivial (tier>0): {non_trivial:,}")
print(f"Mean confidence:      {np.mean(pred_confs):.3f}")
print(f"Median confidence:    {np.median(pred_confs):.3f}")

# Count per tier
from collections import Counter as _Counter14
tier_counts = _Counter14(pred_tiers)
counts = [tier_counts.get(i, 0) for i in range(4)]

fig, (ax_bar, ax_hist) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4.5))

# --- Panel A: tier distribution bar chart ---
bars = ax_bar.bar(TIER_NAMES, counts, color=TIER_PALETTE_LIST,
                  edgecolor="white", width=0.6, zorder=3)
for bar, cnt in zip(bars, counts):
    pct = 100 * cnt / len(pred_tiers)
    ax_bar.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, bar.get_height() + 20,
                f"{cnt:,}\n({pct:.1f}%)", ha="center", va="bottom",
                fontsize=9, fontweight="bold")
ax_bar.set_ylabel("Edge count", fontweight="bold")
ax_bar.set_title("Figure 14.1A \u00b7 Predicted tier distribution (4,001 edges)",
                 fontsize=11)
sns.despine(ax=ax_bar)

# --- Panel B: overlaid confidence histograms ---
for tier_idx in range(4):
    vals = [pred_confs[j] for j in range(len(pred_tiers))
            if pred_tiers[j] == tier_idx]
    if vals:
        ax_hist.hist(vals, bins=30, alpha=0.5,
                     color=TIER_PALETTE_LIST[tier_idx],
                     label=TIER_NAMES[tier_idx], edgecolor="none")
ax_hist.set_xlabel("Confidence", fontweight="bold")
ax_hist.set_ylabel("Frequency", fontweight="bold")
ax_hist.set_title("Figure 14.1B \u00b7 Confidence distribution by tier",
                  fontsize=11)
ax_hist.legend(fontsize=9)
sns.despine(ax=ax_hist)

fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(REPO / "report" / "figures" / "fig14_1_full_graph_predictions.png",
            dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

# Figure 14.2 — Coverage gain heatmap: edges predicted RELATED or EQUIVALENT
proc_edges = _json14.loads(
    (REPO / "data" / "processed" / "edges.json").read_text()
)

# Build lookup: edge_id -> (source_framework, target_framework)
edge_fw = {}
for e in proc_edges:
    edge_fw[e["edge_id"]] = (e["source_framework"], e["target_framework"])

# 9x9 matrix of RELATED (tier>=2) or EQUIVALENT (tier>=3) predictions
fw_list = sorted(FRAMEWORKS)
fw_idx = {f: i for i, f in enumerate(fw_list)}
matrix = np.zeros((len(fw_list), len(fw_list)))

for eid, pred in edge_preds.items():
    if pred["tier"] >= 2 and eid in edge_fw:
        src, tgt = edge_fw[eid]
        if src in fw_idx and tgt in fw_idx:
            matrix[fw_idx[src], fw_idx[tgt]] += 1

pretty_labels = [PRETTY.get(f, f) for f in fw_list]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 7))
sns.heatmap(matrix, annot=True, fmt=".0f", cmap="crest",
            xticklabels=pretty_labels, yticklabels=pretty_labels,
            ax=ax, linewidths=0.5, linecolor="white")
ax.set_title("Figure 14.2 \u00b7 Coverage gain: edges predicted RELATED or EQUIVALENT",
             fontsize=11, pad=12)
plt.setp(ax.get_xticklabels(), rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.setp(ax.get_yticklabels(), rotation=0)
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(REPO / "report" / "figures" / "fig14_2_coverage_gain.png",
            dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

total_strong = int(matrix.sum())
print(f"Edges predicted RELATED or EQUIVALENT: {total_strong:,}")

### Broader impact

The classifier surfaces 416 edges at PARTIAL or above, with 59 predicted EQUIVALENT—concrete candidates for compliance teams mapping between frameworks. These predictions can be contributed back to OpenCRE as draft mappings, closing gaps in the community-maintained graph. The coverage-gain heatmap reveals which framework pairs have the densest predicted relationships, guiding where human reviewers should focus first.

**Limitations.** The frozen test set is small (179 pairs, only 7 EQUIVALENT), so per-class confidence intervals remain wide. RELATED F1 regressed from v7c, indicating the ensemble over-corrects toward rare classes at the expense of mid-tier precision. The hop-distance proxy used for OpenCRE augmentation is crude—semantic similarity between control descriptions would be a better label signal.

**Future work.** A richer CRE topic taxonomy could provide hierarchical priors for the classifier. Expanding the framework catalog to the full OpenCRE collection (50+ standards) would increase training diversity and reduce the domain-adaptation gap.

## 15 · Conclusion

This project traced a complete arc from a hand-tuned composite scorer to a calibrated three-model ensemble. The v7c baseline achieved 81.0% tier accuracy but 0% EQUIVALENT F1—it could not identify the most important class. OpenCRE discovery (Section 10) yielded 6,200 candidate pairs, but v8 disagreement mining and v8b direct augmentation both failed: the first added too few pairs to shift metrics, the second collapsed to single-class prediction. Stripping back to a zero-parameter softmax average over ordinal-loss models produced v_final: 79.9% accuracy, 0.558 macro F1, and 0.400 EQUIVALENT F1—the first time any version detected equivalence.

The failures were as instructive as the successes. Learned stacking overfit under class imbalance; noisy labels poisoned the validation split; DeBERTa-large collapsed without careful regularization. The practical output is 4,001 scored edges across nine AI-security frameworks, each with a tier label and calibrated confidence estimate. These predictions are the foundation for the interactive Dash explorer in Project 2.

> **Plain English:** We started with an 81%-accurate model that could not identify equivalent controls—the most valuable class for compliance teams. We tried adding external data from OpenCRE, broke things twice, then simplified: three models, ordinal losses, averaged probabilities, no learned weights. The result scores 4,001 framework edges with tier labels and confidence values. Those scored edges are the deliverable—a machine-readable crosswalk ready for human review.

---

**Project 2 transition.** The Dash application (Project 2) surfaces these 4,001 scored edges interactively, with classifier tier badges and confidence indicators on every mapping. See [Project 2 (`.`)](.).
